C:
Use a `Set`:

```javascript
function dedup(a){ return [...new Set(a)]; }
```

**Improvement:** O(nВІ) в†’ O(n). The original calls `a.indexOf(x)` for every element, and `indexOf` itself scans up to n elements вЂ” so n elements Г— n-element scan = nВІ comparisons. `Set` membership is amortized O(1), so building the set and spreading it back out is a single linear pass. On a 10k-element array that's roughly 100M comparisons down to 10k operations; the gap widens quadratically as the array grows.

One behavioral difference to know about: the `indexOf` version silently drops every `NaN` (because `indexOf` uses `===`, and `NaN === NaN` is false, so it's never found). The `Set` version keeps one `NaN` (Set uses SameValueZero equality). For all other values вЂ” including `+0`/`-0` and object references вЂ” the two behave identically. If you specifically relied on `NaN` being stripped, that's the only place the output changes.
